title: Origami envelope fold
subtitle: Folding a square sheet into a functional envelope with a triangular flap closure
excerpt: A square sheet of paper folded into an envelope takes under two minutes and produces a functional envelope suited to a note card or a small gift. The fold requires no adhesive: the flap tucks in to close. The envelope fits a card half the size of the original square.
sourceNotes: The square-to-envelope fold is a traditional paper-folding form documented across Japanese and European paper-craft literature. No single primary source; the method described is the standard diagonal-fold envelope taught in origami primers from the mid 20th century onward.

glossaryTerms.definition: A fold that runs from one corner of a square or rectangle to the opposite corner, creating a crease along the diagonal axis. The diagonal fold is the starting point of many origami forms including the envelope, the water bomb base, and the preliminary base.

A method of closing an envelope or container by inserting the flap into the pocket formed by the folded body, rather than using adhesive or a sealing method. The tuck relies on the layers of paper holding each other in place through friction.

content.text: The origami envelope is one of the most practical paper-fold forms: it requires one square sheet and produces a functional envelope with a

. A 20 cm square produces an envelope that holds an A6 (or 10 x 15 cm) card. A 15 cm square holds a small note. Use a patterned or decorated sheet for an attractive result: the inside of the envelope shows only white, while the outside shows the full pattern.
Place the sheet colour-side down, oriented as a diamond (one corner pointing toward you, one corner pointing away). Make the
: fold the bottom corner up to the top corner. Crease and unfold. This is a reference crease only; unfold fully.
Fold the bottom corner up to the centre crease intersection (the midpoint of the sheet). Crease flat. This forms the bottom of the envelope pocket.

Fold the left corner inward to the centre: the left point moves to the midline. Crease. Fold the right corner inward in the same way. The two folded edges should meet exactly at the centre vertical line, forming a long narrow diamond.
Fold the top point down approximately three quarters of the way toward the bottom point. The exact distance determines the depth of the flap: a shorter fold gives a deeper pocket. Crease.

Fold the top point back up approximately one third of the way, creating a triangular flap with a clean horizontal bottom edge. Crease sharply. This is the flap that will tuck into the envelope to close it.

Unfold the flap to open the envelope. Insert your note or card. Fold the flap down and tuck its point into the pocket formed between the side flaps. The flap should sit flat and the envelope should hold closed without adhesive.

For a sealed envelope, apply a small dot of glue stick or acid-free PVA to the flap before tucking. For a decorative closure instead, add a small sticker seal or tie the envelope closed with ribbon or a thread wrap rather than tucking. Using origami washi or duo paper gives a finer finish and crisper creases than standard kami, which may be worth the slight extra cost for gift enclosures.
